one_line_takeaway: A conceptual manifesto arguing that security of interacting AI agents is non-compositional and requires a new discipline (MASEC), delivering an extended multi-agent attacker model, a ~10-category threat taxonomy, an open-problems research agenda, and a three-layer governance framework -- but no empirical evaluation of its own.
key_finding: The paper's central argued finding is non-compositionality: individually safe agents can compose into unsafe systems, and this is not merely a prediction failure but a fundamental limitation on observability because covert coordination (e.g., steganographic collusion, illusory attacks) can be statistically indistinguishable from benign interaction even under full communication visibility. From this it derives that passive monitoring/oversight that does not modify agent communications may be defeated by provably-secure steganography, motivating secure-by-design bounds on harm and decision-theoretic detection of systemic consequences rather than of the communications themselves. It further argues these dynamics create novel systemic and national-security risks (swarm attacks, cascade attacks, heterogeneous attacks that chain 'safe' agents to bypass safeguards) that fall outside existing single-agent AI security, MAS security, cryptographic, and game-theoretic threat models.
core_contribution: A position/framing paper that proposes 'multi-agent security' (MASEC) as a distinct discipline dedicated to threats that emerge or amplify through interactions between AI agents (direct communication or shared-environment manipulation), rather than the single-agent, model-centric focus of existing AI security. It offers a formal definition of multi-agent systems (Definition 1.1) and of multi-agent security (Definition 1.2), and states three concrete deliverables (Section 1, 'Contributions'): (1) an extended attacker model plus a threat taxonomy of challenges unique to or amplified by multi-agent settings (Section 3, Table 1); (2) a directory of open research problems spanning environment design, provenance, protocols, monitoring, containment, and attribution (Section 4); and (3) a three-layer governance framework for multi-agent systems (Section 5). The paper is explicitly conceptual and anticipatory: it aims to characterize threat classes that are structurally enabled by multi-agent interaction but 'not yet widely observed in practice,' contrasting this with reactive, ad-hoc patching.
threat_model: The paper builds its OWN attacker model on top of classical computer-security threat modeling (Saltzer & Schroeder; Anderson) and extends it with axes specific to agentic/multi-agent settings (Section 3.1). Attackers range from state actors and criminal organizations to security researchers, ideological groups, corporate competitors, and even unintentionally misbehaving agents. Proposed new axes include: source of intent (externally specified vs. emergent from learned/misaligned policies vs. hybrid delegated autonomy); degree of delegated autonomy; the attacker's position in the agent graph; influence radius across agents; control over goal specification; and access to shared state and memory. A central claim is that the effective attacker may not be a single corrupted node but an expanding, colluding coalition of agents that jointly manipulate goals, memory, or communication, making attribution and the unit of analysis fundamentally harder than in single-agent threat models. The overarching thesis is that security in multi-agent systems is non-compositional: individually safe agents can compose into unsafe systems, and coordination/information flow can be embedded so as to be indistinguishable from benign interaction even under full observability of communication.
limitations: No empirical validation of any kind: no datasets, no experiments, no measured defense effectiveness; proposed defenses (research agenda, governance framework) are described but not tested. The work is explicitly anticipatory, so many threat classes are 'structurally enabled but not yet widely observed,' making them hard to falsify or prioritize. The taxonomy is self-described as non-exhaustive and pragmatic rather than theoretically rigorous, and is a v1.0 living document soliciting future contributions. Much of the paper (Section 2) is a broad related-work survey; a reviewer must be careful not to attribute surveyed results (secret-collusion success rates, M-FOS payoffs, benchmark numbers, Microsoft/MITRE/OWASP taxonomy counts) to this paper's own contribution. The governance and sociotechnical framing is deliberately broad ('any entity or process that can affect or be affected by the agent society'), which risks diluting a concrete, testable threat model.

quantitative_results: Own structural contribution: a threat taxonomy (Table 1) of roughly 10 multi-agent security challenge categories -- Privacy and Information Integrity, Collusion and Exfiltration, Exploitation, Swarm Attacks, Heterogeneous Attacks, Oversight Attacks, Cascade Attacks, Conflict and Social Dilemmas, Physical and Embodied Attacks, and Sociotechnical Threats.

Own structural contribution: three stated contributions (extended attacker model + taxonomy in Section 3; open-problems directory across environment design, provenance, protocols, monitoring, containment, attribution in Section 4; three-layer governance framework in Section 5).

Attacker-model extension proposes multiple new axes for multi-agent settings (source of intent, delegated autonomy, position in agent graph, influence radius, control over goal specification, access to shared state/memory).

CITED (not the authors' own experiment): Microsoft's failure-mode taxonomy identifies 27 categories from red-teaming, including 'multi-agent jailbreaks' and 'agent flow manipulation' (Bryan et al., 2025); Reid et al. (2025) identify six multi-agent governance failure modes; OWASP ranks prompt injection as the top LLM threat. These are surveyed in Section 2, not measured here.
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines (non-empirical paper). It positions/differentiates itself CONCEPTUALLY against related frameworks: the MAESTRO operational taxonomy (Huang 2025) which it says it complements/extends; the Cooperative AI Foundation Multi-Agent Risks report (Hammond et al. 2025); MITRE ATLAS agent techniques; Microsoft's 27-category failure-mode taxonomy; and concurrent multi-agent-focused work (He et al. 2025a compositional vulnerabilities; Reid et al. 2025; Sharma et al. 2025 agent cascading injection; Deng et al. 2026 agentic web; Darius et al. 2025 'Agentology'). Its claimed differentiator is an anticipatory, interaction-centric, sociotechnical posture vs. these adversary-/architecture-centric or already-observed catalogs.
